title: Stage 2: MiroFish reasoning
subtitle: The broad graph tightens once MiroFish layers in real relationship memory, venture fit, and the lowest-friction ask.
summary: Tigerclaw ranks the broad network first. MiroFish then narrows it into a path replay that answers the founder question directly: who should I message first, what proof shifts trust, what risk makes the ask feel too early, and what exact next step keeps the relationship warm rather than transactional.
callouts: The primary path is now direct and venture-adjacent: Harrison at Hack VC becomes the best person to ask because the route is already warm and the ask can stay narrow.
The reasoning uses more than LinkedIn profile text. It adds manual founder memory like recent conversations and shared AI-agent context that LinkedIn does not capture cleanly.
The next stage shows the softer friend-first route through Katia into Justin at Decasonic, proving the system can choose connectors before capital allocators.

connector.name: Harrison Dahme
connector.title: CTO @ Hack VC | Engineering & Ventures | Agent Framework & Blockchain Lab Co-Founder

likelyInvestorDestination.name: Hack VC venture path
likelyInvestorDestination.description: A warm AI, infrastructure, and crypto-adjacent investor route that stays specific instead of broad.

narrative.warmPathSummary: Tigerclaw identified Harrison as the strongest current investor-facing route because the relationship is already warm, the venture context is direct, and the first ask can be framed as a focused calibration conversation instead of an immediate fundraising request.
narrative.whyThisPerson: Harrison is compelling because the path is already venture-adjacent and the relationship carries real founder memory: recent AI-agent and infrastructure discussion, shared operator language, and enough trust to reopen the thread naturally.
narrative.whyNow: The timing works because the founder can follow up with a short Hack VC fit memo and a traction snapshot while the conversation context is still recent, which makes the next step feel like a continuation rather than a cold ask.
narrative.whyThisRankedAboveOthers: It ranked above softer bridge candidates because the route ends closer to actual capital allocation, yet still preserves warmth. It ranked above a direct Justin-first route because Harrison allows a narrower, lower-friction re-entry into the venture conversation.
narrative.suggestedAsk: Message Harrison with a short context refresh, attach a concise Hack VC fit memo plus a Tigerclaw traction snapshot, and ask for a brief calibration call before requesting any broader investor path.
supportingProof: Recent venture conversation around AI agents, infrastructure, and crypto-adjacent product strategy.
A short Tigerclaw traction snapshot and investor-fit memo tailored to Hack VC.
Shared operator context that makes the message feel like a continuation of an existing conversation instead of a generic founder pitch.
narrative.likelyObjection: If the outreach jumps too fast to a broad fundraise intro, the warmth of the relationship can collapse into a transactional ask.
narrative.nextBestStep: Send a context-first reply, keep the ask to a short calibration conversation, and only widen it into investor routing after the fit is confirmed.
narrative.draftMessage: Hey Harrison, great chatting recently about AI-agent infrastructure and crypto-adjacent product strategy. I am building Tigerclaw, which helps founders turn their existing network into the warmest investor path, and I think there is a specific overlap with the kind of founder and infrastructure context Hack VC sees. I put together a very short note on why I think the fit is real, plus a concise traction snapshot. Would you be open to a quick calibration call, and if it resonates, point me toward the right next investor path rather than me guessing from the outside?

executionRows.label: Route mode
executionRows.value: Primary direct venture route via Harrison into Hack VC.

executionRows.label: Draft posture
executionRows.value: Keep the first message calibration-led, specific, and context-first.
